evidence_quotes.quote: Video game companies are abusing their power in deciding what happens with the games that consumers have paid for and bought, especially in that access to those games can end, and in many cases already has.

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act's proposed elements already contain a mention about video games, so I wanted to add my thoughts about something else that concerns video games and should be a part of this initiative. Video game companies are abusing their power in deciding what happens with the games that consumers have paid for and bought, especially in that access to those games can end, and in many cases already has. This happens with consumers buying a licence to a video game that has not been advertised as being a service that has an expiration date. To the consumer it is a clear cut purchase of access to using a product. The company who produced or published the product one sidedly decides down the line that they no longer wish to support it, which causes the consumer to lose access to something they paid for. This is not fair. These companies have an incentive to stop supporting and remove access to their older games in order to potentially make their new release the only way to keep playing a certain type of game, even though the new release may not be that much different from the original release that the consumer already paid for. This practice is inherently unfair for the consumer and there needs to be enforcement to make sure that consumers can own what they have purchased instead of companies selling something and then taking away what they sold and still keeping the money. One recent example of this happening is the game called The Crew (released in 2014), which was shut down in March of 2024. This game had a single player mode that does not require internet access, but even still after the shutdown of online services for that game, no one who bought it can play any part of the game any more. We don't need these companies to support the games forever. We only ask that they keep the games functional. I hope that this feedback is taken into account when preparing the final version of Digital Fairness Act.
